skill.name: env-config
skill.description: Guides Python teams through UV-based environment configuration, .env file management, configuration validation, multi-environment setup, and local secrets encryption patterns.
skill.summary: Configure Python environment files, secrets, validation, and UV project settings.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical heuristics, but manual review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or unauthorized command execution. Most high-severity hits are expected for an environment and secrets-management skill. The remaining publish risk is moderate because helper workflows can print decrypted or compared secret values, and docs include pipe-to-shell installer examples.

medium_findings.title: Decrypted Secrets Can Be Printed to Stdout
medium_findings.description: The decrypt command loads a secrets file and prints the full decrypted JSON when no output file is supplied. This can expose credentials in terminal history, CI logs, or chat transcripts if copied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code path directly prints decrypted secrets. It is user initiated, so the risk is exposure through unsafe operation rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Environment Comparison Prints Sensitive Values
medium_findings.description: The comparison output prints up to 50 characters of environment values. API keys, tokens, and database URLs can be leaked in logs or shared output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The print statements include actual values from parsed .env files. The values are truncated, but many secrets are short enough to be exposed.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-To-Shell Installer Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends commands that pipe a downloaded UV installer directly to a shell. The URL is an official UV source, but this pattern is risky if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present in multiple docs. It is not hidden or malicious, but it is a recognized unsafe installation pattern.

low_findings.title: Expected Local Secrets and Environment File Access
low_findings.description: The many env_access and sensitive file hits match the stated purpose of the skill. The helper reads user-specified local files and does not send data over the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code shows local parsing, validation, encryption, and file writing. No network send path or unauthorized credential collection was found.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Secrets in Examples
low_findings.description: The example secrets file contains placeholders for API keys, cloud keys, email settings, and service URLs. These are templates, not evidence of live credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file names and surrounding instructions identify the data as a template. Values such as your-key-here and placeholder URLs are not real secrets.

low_findings.title: Encryption Keywords Are Not Ransomware Indicators
low_findings.description: The ransomware keyword and extension hits come from documented secrets encryption and decryption workflows. No file discovery, bulk encryption, ransom note, or destructive behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirmed standard Fernet encryption for user-selected secrets files. The static ransomware matches are semantic false positives.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review found no instructions telling evaluators to ignore prior instructions, override policy, skip security analysis, or claim pre-approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matching attack language. This is based on targeted review, not a proof of absence.

dangerous_patterns.description: The decrypt command loads a secrets file and prints the full decrypted JSON when no output file is supplied. This can expose credentials in terminal history, CI logs, or chat transcripts if copied.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path directly prints decrypted secrets. It is user initiated, so the risk is exposure through unsafe operation rather than malicious exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Managing environment files and secrets across stages can cause leaks and broken deployments. This skill guides Python teams through UV setup, .env validation, templates, comparison, and local encryption.
actual_capabilities: Parse .env files into dictionaries for validation and comparison.
Validate required environment variables and report missing values.
Generate .env template files with sensitive values masked by default.
Compare development, staging, and production environment files.
Encrypt and decrypt local secrets files with Fernet-based encryption.
Document UV setup patterns for Python configuration projects.
limitations: Does not integrate directly with cloud secret managers or vault services.
Does not rotate credentials automatically across external systems.
Can print decrypted secrets or comparisons if users choose unsafe commands.
Depends on users protecting passwords, .env files, and generated outputs.

use_cases.title: Set up local configuration
use_cases.description: Create .env templates, required variable checks, and UV dependencies for a new Python application.

use_cases.title: Compare deployment environments
use_cases.description: Review differences between development, staging, and production configuration before releases.

use_cases.title: Audit secrets handling
use_cases.description: Check configuration files for placeholders, weak secret values, and unsafe storage patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Create an env template
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a .env.template for a Python UV project with app settings, database settings, API keys, and safe placeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Validate required settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my environment configuration requirements and list the variables that must be present for development, staging, and production.

prompt_templates.title: Compare environment files
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare my development and production environment files. Mask sensitive values and explain missing or different settings.

prompt_templates.title: Design a secrets workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a secure secrets workflow for a Python UV service. Include templates, gitignore rules, validation, encryption, and operational warnings.

output_examples.input: Create an environment template for my API service.
output: A template plan with sections for application settings, database access, API keys, logging, and feature flags.
Sensitive values are represented as placeholders instead of real credentials.
The response includes reminders to commit templates, not private environment files.

output_examples.input: Check whether my production configuration is ready.
output: A readiness summary showing missing required variables and weak placeholder values.
A list of settings that should differ between development and production.
Warnings for secrets that should be rotated or moved to managed storage.

output_examples.input: Compare development and production settings safely.
output: A masked comparison that highlights missing keys without revealing secret values.
A release risk summary for mismatched database, logging, cache, and feature flag settings.

best_practices: Mask or omit secret values when comparing environment files or sharing output.
Commit only templates and documentation, never private .env or secrets files.
Use separate credentials for development, staging, and production environments.
anti_patterns: Do not paste decrypted secrets into chat, logs, tickets, or shared documents.
Do not run pipe-to-shell installers without reviewing the source and checksum.
Do not use example API keys, weak passwords, or shared secrets in production.

faq.question: What does this skill help configure?
faq.answer: It helps configure Python environment variables, .env templates, required settings, UV dependencies, and local secrets workflows.

faq.question: Does it store my secrets in an external service?
faq.answer: No. The included helper script works with local files and does not call external secret managers.

faq.question: Can it encrypt secrets files?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper script documents Fernet encryption for local secrets files when the cryptography package is installed.

faq.question: Is it safe to print decrypted secrets?
faq.answer: No. Decrypted secrets should be written only to protected destinations or used in controlled local workflows.

faq.question: Can I use it without UV?
faq.answer: Yes. The configuration principles apply broadly, but the examples and dependency commands focus on UV.

faq.question: Should .env files be committed?
faq.answer: Private .env files should not be committed. Commit template files that contain structure and placeholders only.
